title: Development of catecholaminergic neurons in the pond snail,Lymnaea stagnalis: I. Embryonic development of dopamine-containing neurons and dopamine-dependent behaviors
abstract:
Development of Catecholaminergic
Neurons in the Pond Snail, Lymnaea
stagnalis: I. Embryonic Development
of Dopamine-Containing Neurons
and Dopamine-Dependent Behaviors
ELENA E. VORONEZHSKAYA,1 LA´ SZLO´ HIRIPI,2 KA´ ROLY ELEKES,2
AND ROGER P. CROLL3*
1Institute of Developmental Biology, Russian Academy of Sciences, 117808 Moscow, Russia
2Department of Experimental Zoology, Balaton Limnological Research Institute,
Hungarian Academy of Science, H-8237 Tihany, Hungary
3Department of Physiology and Biophysics, Faculty of Medicine, Dalhousie University,
Halifax, Nova Scotia B3H 4H7, Canada
ABSTRACT
The embryonic development of the catecholaminergic system of the pond snail, Lymnaea
stagnalis, was investigated by using chromatographic and histochemical methods. High
performance liquid chromatography suggested that dopamine was the only catecholamine
present in signiﬁcant concentrations throughout the embryonic development of Lymnaea.
Dopamine ﬁrst became detectable at about embryonic stage (E) 15 (15% of embryonic
development) and then increased in amount during early development to reach about 120–140
fmol per animal by around E40. Dopamine content remained stable during mid-embryogen-
esis (E40–65), increased slowing for the next couple of days, and then increased rapidly to
culminate at about 400 fmol per animal by hatching. The detection of aldehyde- and
glyoxylate-induced ﬂuorescence and of tyrosine hydroxylaselike immunoreactivity indicated
that the ﬁrst catecholaminergic cells appeared in the late trochophore or early veliger stage of
embryonic development (E32–35). The paired perikarya of these transient apical catecholamin-
ergic (TAC) neurons were located beneath the apical plate, remained outside of the central
ganglia during embryogenesis, and no longer contained detectable catecholamines close to
hatching. TAC neurons bore cilia on the ends of short processes that penetrated the overlying
epithelium; their long processes branched repeatedly under the ciliated apical plate. Several
smaller catecholaminergic cells ﬁrst appeared in the anterior margin of the foot at a stage
when the embryos began to metamorphose from the veliger form (E55). Similar bipolar cells
later appeared in the tentacle and lips. The axons of all of these small peripheral cells
projected centrally and terminated within the neuropil of different central ganglia. Central
catecholaminergic neurons, including RPeD1, differentiated only after metamorphosis was
complete (E75). Development of locomotor, respiratory, and feeding behaviors correlated with
maturation of catecholaminergic neurons, as indicated by histology and chromatography. J.
Comp. Neurol. 404:285–296, 1999.
r 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Indexing terms: gastropod; mollusc; monoamine; tyrosine hydroxylase
Although the basic mechanisms and timing of ganglio-
genesis in gastropods have been known for about half a
century (for review, see Raven, 1966), more recent studies
have added important details and improved our under-
standing of events during development. Such studies have
demonstrated some of the ﬁrst neurons to differentiate and
have permitted the visualization of some of the earliest
Grant sponsor: Hungarian Scientiﬁc Research Fund (OTKA); Grant
numbers: 6284 and 23472; Grant sponsor: Natural Sciences and Engineer-
ing Research Council (NSERC) of Canada; Grant number: OGP0038863.
*Correspondence to: Roger P. Croll, Department of Physiology and
Biophysics, Faculty of Medicine, Dalhousie University, Halifax, Nova
Scotia B3H 4H7, Canada. E-mail: roger.croll@dal.ca
Received 20 March 1998; Revised 15 September 1998; Accepted 22
September 1998
THE JOURNAL OF COMPARATIVE NEUROLOGY 404:285–296 (1999)
r 1999 WILEY-LISS, INC.
 axonal pathways (Schacher et al., 1979; Kandel et al.,
1981; Goldberg and Kater, 1989; Marois and Croll, 1992;
Croll and Voronezhskaya, 1995, 1996a,b; Marois and Carew,
1997b; Kempf et al., 1997). Such studies have also con-
ﬁrmed previously hypothesized origins of the developing
nervous system and have indicated possible routes by
which cells migrate from peripheral proliferative zones to
the central ganglia (MacAllister et al., 1983; Jacob, 1984;
Hickmott and Carew 1991). Many of these studies have
relied on markers for different biologically active sub-
stances to label speciﬁc subsets of neurons that could
conveniently be followed through subsequent develop-
ment. The ontogeny of gastropod neurons, which contain
serotonin (5-HT; Croll and Chaisson, 1989; Goldberg and
Kater, 1989; Barlow and Truman, 1992; Marois and Croll,
1992; Voronezhskaya and Elekes, 1993; Kempf et al., 1997;
Marois and Carew, 1997a,b), ovulation hormones (Mac-
Allister et al., 1983), Phe-Met-Arg-Phe-amide (FMR-
Famide; Kempf et al., 1987, 1992; Croll and Voronezhs-
kaya, 1995; Voronezhskaya and Elekes, 1996), small
cardioactive peptides (Kempf et al., 1987; Barlow and
Truman, 1992), octopamine (Elekes et al., 1996), and
NADPH diaphorase (Lin and Leise, 1996; Serfozo et al.,
1998), therefore, have been well described. However, little
is known about the development of catecholaminergic
neurons in gastropods.
Catecholamines (CAs) are abundant in the nervous
systems of molluscs, where they play different regulatory
roles (Walker, 1986). In postlarval life, CAs have been
implicated in the control of several functions such as
feeding (Wieland and Gelperin, 1983; Trimble and Barker,
1984; Kyriakides and McCrohan, 1989; Kemenes et al.,
1990; Teyke et al., 1993), respiration (Syed et al., 1990,
1991; Syed and Winlow, 1991), locomotion (Sakharov and
Sala´nki, 1982), and growth (de Vlieger et al., 1986). In
addition, CAs have been suggested to act at the earliest
developmental stages as signals inﬂuencing cell prolifera-
tion, differentiation, and neuritogenesis in different mol-
luscs (Buznikov, 1987; Buznikov et al., 1996; Spencer et al.,
1996). CAs have also been suggested to play a role in the
control of larval settling and metamorphosis in free-living
marine larvae (Hadﬁeld, 1984; Coon et al., 1985; Coon and
Bonar, 1986; Pires et al., 1997) and of metamorphosis
during the intracapsular development of pond snails (Vor-
onezhskaya et al., 1992).
In the present and companion (Croll et al., 1998) studies,
we present a comprehensive description of the ontogeny of
catecholaminergic (CAergic) neurons from their ﬁrst ap-
pearance in the embryo of the pond snail, Lymnaea
stagnalis, through postembryonic development. We in-
clude descriptions of the projection patterns of both periph-
eral and central neurons and high performance liquid
chromatographic (HPLC) analysis of the CA content dur-
ing embryogenesis. In addition, the present article, which
describes the embryonic development, also provides data
on the correlation between the ontogeny of speciﬁc CAergic
cells and cell groups and the development of certain
behaviors that have previously been shown to involve CAs
in adult snails.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Animals
Egg masses were collected from laboratory populations
of Lymnaea stagnalis maintained in Canada, Hungary,
and Russia. The embryonic development was staged on the
basis of a speciﬁc set of morphological, morphometric, and
behavioral features according to Mescheriakov (1990) and
Morrill (1982). Stages were expressed as a percentage of
total embryonic development, according to Marois and
Croll (1992), wherein 0% [embryonic stage (E) 0] corre-
sponds to the ﬁrst egg cleavage and 100% (E100) to
hatching.
HPLC
The modiﬁed method of McCaman et al. (1972, 1973)
was used for the detection and quantiﬁcation of mono-
amines. Either 50 early (E15–45) embryos or 20 older
(E50–95) embryos were removed from their egg capsules
and then homogenized in 300 µl of 0.1 M phosphate buffer
(PB), pH 7.0, containing isoproterenol as an internal
standard. The homogenate was then centrifuged at 6,000g
for 15 minutes.Amines were isolated from an aliquot of the
supernatant with 300 µl chloroform containing 0.1 M
di-(2-etylhexyl)-phosphoric acid. After thorough mixing,
all the tubes were centrifuged, and the overlying aqueous
phase was aspirated and discarded. The organic phase was
washed with 0.1 M PB, pH 7.0. A 250-µl aliquot of organic
phase was removed and added to 80 µl of 0.1 N HCl in a
fresh tube. The tubes were mixed vigorously and then
centrifuged. The overlying aqueous phase was aspirated
into the microtube of a Waters Corp. (Milford, MA) sample
injector (WISP712), and a 50-µl aliquot was injected into
the Waters HPLC system, consisting of a pump (M510), an
electrochemical detector (M460), and an integrator (M746).
The amines were separated on a 125/4-mm Nucleosil 5C18
reverse/phase column (Macherey-Nagel GmbH & Co. KG,
Dueren, Germany). The mobile phase consisted of 0.1 M
PB (pH 4.0), containing 1 mM octane sulfonic acid, 1 mM
EDTA, and 10% methanol. The ﬂow rate was 1 ml/minute,
and the potential on the detector was set at 10.65 V.
Immunocytochemistry
At least 50 embryos at each of E20, E40, E60, E90, and
E100 stages were removed from egg capsules and shells
and were placed immediately into 4% paraformaldehyde
diluted in 0.1 M PB. After 4 hours of ﬁxation at 4°C the
embryos were washed for 12 hours in phosphate buffered
saline (PBS; 50 mM Na2HPO4 and 140 mM NaCl, pH 7.2)
with 4% Triton X-100 and then processed for immunohisto-
chemistry according to either the two-step ﬂuorescence
method of Coons et al. (1941) as modiﬁed by Marois and
Croll (1992) or the three-step peroxidase anti-peroxidase
(PAP) method of Sternberger (1979) as modiﬁed by Voron-
ezhskaya and Elekes (1993). All incubations were per-
formed at 4°C for 24–48 hours.
For immunoﬂuorescence, the ﬁxed tissues were incu-
bated for 36 hours with a monoclonal, anti-tyrosine hy-
droxylase (TH) IgG (Incstar, Stillwater, MN) diluted 1:100
in PBS with 5% normal sheep serum and 4% Triton X-100
(PBS-TX). After several rinses in PBS, the tissues were
incubated for 8 hours in sheep anti-mouse IgG (1:50)
conjugated with either ﬂuorescein isothiocyanate (FITC)
or rhodamine (Jackson ImmunoResearch Laboratories,
Inc., West Grove, PA). After several more rinses, the
tissues were mounted in the 3:1 mixture of glycerol and
PBS and then viewed and photographed by using the
appropriate ﬁlter blocks (I3, L3 or N2.1) on a Leitz
Aristoplan microscope.
286
E.E. VORONEZHSKAYA ET AL.

Dueren, Germany). The mobile phase consisted of 0.1 M
PB (pH 4.0), containing 1 mM octane sulfonic acid, 1 mM
EDTA, and 10% methanol. The ﬂow rate was 1 ml/minute,
and the potential on the detector was set at 10.65 V.

E100 stages were removed from egg capsules and shells
and were placed immediately into 4% paraformaldehyde
diluted in 0.1 M PB. After 4 hours of ﬁxation at 4°C the
embryos were washed for 12 hours in phosphate buffered
saline (PBS; 50 mM Na2HPO4 and 140 mM NaCl, pH 7.2)
with 4% Triton X-100 and then processed for immunohisto-
chemistry according to either the two-step ﬂuorescence
method of Coons et al. (1941) as modiﬁed by Marois and
Croll (1992) or the three-step peroxidase anti-peroxidase
(PAP) method of Sternberger (1979) as modiﬁed by Voron-
ezhskaya and Elekes (1993). All incubations were per-
formed at 4°C for 24–48 hours.
For immunoﬂuorescence, the ﬁxed tissues were incu-
bated for 36 hours with a monoclonal, anti-tyrosine hy-
droxylase (TH) IgG (Incstar, Stillwater, MN) diluted 1:100
in PBS with 5% normal sheep serum and 4% Triton X-100
(PBS-TX). After several rinses in PBS, the tissues were
incubated for 8 hours in sheep anti-mouse IgG (1:50)
conjugated with either ﬂuorescein isothiocyanate (FITC)
or rhodamine (Jackson ImmunoResearch Laboratories,
Inc., West Grove, PA). After several more rinses, the
tissues were mounted in the 3:1 mixture of glycerol and
PBS and then viewed and photographed by using the
appropriate ﬁlter blocks (I3, L3 or N2.1) on a Leitz
For detection of immunoreactivity with PAP, the ﬁxed
tissues were ﬁrst incubated for 1 hour in 2% H2O2 in PB to
block the endogenous peroxidase activity. The tissues were
washed and then incubated with the primary antibody, as
described above. After several rinses, the tissues were
incubated for 8 hours in unlabeled sheep anti-mouse IgG
(1:50; Dakopatts, Glostrup, Denmark), rinsed again, and
then incubated for 2 hours in PAP (1:50; Dakopatts). After
several additional rinses, the tissue samples were incu-
bated for 20 minutes in 0.05% 3,38-diaminobenzidine
(DAB; Sigma Chemical Corp., St. Louis, MO) diluted in
0.05 M Tris-HCl (pH 7.4). The histochemical reaction was
developed by adding 0.01% H2O2 for 3–6 minutes. Tissues
were then dehydrated through a graded alcohol series,
cleared with methyl salicylate, mounted in Canada-
balsam, viewed, and photographed with a Zeiss Axioplan
On several occasions, preparations were also double
labeled with a polyclonal antibody raised against detyrosi-
nated a-tubulin (Jackson et al., 1995) that was demon-
strated subsequently by FITC-labeled goat anti-rabbit IgG
(Jackson ImmunoResearch Laboratories) and the monoclo-
nal anti-TH antibody and by either rhodamine-labeled
sheep anti-mouse IgG or via the PAP procedures described
above. These double-labeled preparations were examined
either on the Leitz Aristoplan microscope or on a Zeiss
Axiovert microscope equipped for confocal laser scanning
(model LSM 410). In the latter case, photographs were
produced by superpositioning stacks of 10–15 images
obtained through stepped sequences of focal planes at
Control experiments involved the use of identical proce-
dures except for the replacement of the primary antiserum
with either 1% normal mouse serum or with the serum
dilutant (PBS-TX) alone. No staining was observed after
any of these control preparations.
At least 30 embryos at each 5% interval of development
were removed from their egg capsules and incubated with
glyoxylic acid for the histoﬂuorescent demonstration of
monoamines according to De la Torre (1980), with some
modiﬁcations introduced by Kabotyanski and Sakharov
(1989) for wholemount molluscan tissues. Whole embryos
were used to examine earlier embryonic stages (E20–35).
For advanced stages (E40–100), partial dissections pro-
vided better staining and visualization. Brieﬂy, the tissues
were immersed in a freshly prepared, buffered glyoxylic
acid–sucrose solution (500 mM sodium glyoxylate, 150 mM
sucrose, 50 mM Tris buffer, pH 7.4) at 4°C. After 30–90
minutes of incubation, the tissues were removed from the
solution and positioned on glass slides. The surrounding
solution was removed, and the tissues were then air dried
at room temperature for 15–30 minutes. Preparations
were then heated to 60°C for 30 minutes. Two to three
drops of paraffin oil were put on the tissue before place-
ment of a top cover slip. The tissues were viewed and
photographed by using a Leitz Aristoplan microscope
equipped for ultraviolet epiﬂuorescence (D ﬁlter block).
The presence of catecholamines in embryonic cells was
conﬁrmed by using the formaldehyde–glutaraldehyde
(FaGlu) method of Furness et al. (1977), as modiﬁed by
Croll and Chiasson (1990) for wholemount molluscan
tissues. Brieﬂy, the embryos were ﬁxed for 1–3 hours in a
freshly prepared solution of 4% paraformaldehyde and
0.5% glutaraldehyde in 0.1 M PB adjusted to pH 7.4. The
tissues were then either air dried on slides and mounted in
3:1 glycerol in PBS or dehydrated quickly through alcohol,
cleared in methyl salicylate, and mounted in Fluoromount
(BDH, Poole, UK). These preparations were also viewed
and photographed by a Leitz Aristoplan microscope
equipped for ultraviolet epiﬂuorescence (D ﬁlter block).
Histological preparations were photographed with Ko-
dak TMAX 100 ﬁlm. Printed photographs were digitally
scanned, and the images were labeled and assembled into
plates by using Photoshop 3.0 (Adobe Systems, Inc., San
Jose, CA). Contrast and brightness of the images were
adjusted to provide consistency within plates.
Gross embryonic movements (rotation, crawling, large
body contractions) were recorded by using an aparatus in
which the images of single living embryos in their egg
capsules were projected onto a photovoltaic cell (Labas et
al., 1982). The resulting electrical signals were subse-
quently displayed on a Hewlett-Packard pen recorder
(HP-30C). These behaviors and the simultaneous rasping
movements of the buccal mass were also visually observed
through a microscope. A manual switch permitted their
display on a parallel channel of the recorder during the
15–20 minutes of observation period. Respiratory behav-
iors (pneumostome opening and closing) were also ob-
served visually and either displayed on chart recordings or
simply counted over 5-minute intervals. The behaviors of
at least 20 embryos taken from different egg masses were
examined for each developmental stage reported.
Dopamine (DA) and 5-HT were the major monoamines
found in Lymnaea throughout embryogenesis, whereas no
epinephrine or norepinephrine were detected in the HPLC
assays (Fig. 1A–C). Figure 1D summarizes the mean
amounts of DA detected in embryos at different stages of
development. DA was ﬁrst detectable in low concentra-
tions at around stage E15 and increased gradually in
amount from stages E25 to E45 (49.2–113.4 fmol/animal);
between stages E45 and E80, the DA content remained
constant (137.0–156.6 fmol/animal). A rapid increase in
DA content (from 184.5 to 415.3 fmol/animal) occurred
during the last day of embryonic development.
Appearance and distribution of the
The two- and the three-step immunohistochemical meth-
ods produced similar patterns of immunostaining in youn-
ger embryos. In later developmental stages, however, the
DAB reaction product was often masked by endogenous
pigment granules of the wholemounted embryos. Simi-
larly, patterns of glyoxylate- and FaGlu-induced blue-
green ﬂuorescence were both consistent and matched well
with the distribution of the TH-like immunoreactive
(TH-lir) elements throughout most of embryonic develop-
CATECHOLAMINERGIC CELLS IN EMBRYONIC SNAILS
ment. However, by E95, certain cells appeared that exhib-
ited glyoxylate- and FaGlu-induced ﬂuorescence but were
not immunoreactive to the antibody raised against TH.
These blue-green ﬂuorescing but immunonegative neu-
rons are discussed later in the text.
The ﬁrst CAergic cells were detected at stages E32–35 of
embryonic development (a late trochophore or very early
veliger stage). At this point, two bilaterally symmetric
10–15-µm cells were located dorsolateral to the mouth
opening along what appeared to be the inner surface of the
developing body wall (Fig. 2A) of all specimens examined
(N 5 380). One or two long processes originated medially
from each of the cells. These axon processes ramiﬁed
repeatedly to form a network of varicose ﬁbers underneath
the ciliated apical plate (Fig. 2B,C). A single, stout process
extending laterally from each soma traversed the epithe-
lium and terminated as a tuft of cilia at the border of apical
and cephalic plates (Fig. 2D,E). As will be described below,
these ﬁrst CAergic neurons ceased to be detectable before
hatching; therefore, we refer to them as transient apical
The paired TAC cells remained as the only CAergic
neurons in the embryos throughout the entire veliger
stage of development. Only at the onset of metamorphosis
(around E55) did the next CAergic neurons begin to appear
in the periphery (Fig. 3A). By this time, three CAergic
somata appeared in the anterior region on each side of the
foot. By E65, the number of these neurons had increased
(Figs. 3B,C, 4A), with the cells often appearing as doublets.
Similar cells could also be observed around the mouth (see
Fig. 4A,B), and a single CAergic soma was present in each
of the developing tentacles (Fig. 3D). All these cells were
small (5–8 µm) and bipolar, with a short apical dendrite
that projected to the surface through the epithelium
(Fig. 3B). The long, axonal processes from neighboring
groups of these cells formed bundles running to the central
ganglia, which had already started to be formed by this
time (Raven, 1966; Cumin, 1972; Morrill, 1982; Mescheria-
kov, 1990). The processes from the bipolar cells in the lips
and tentacles projected to neuropilar regions of the ipsilat-
eral cerebral ganglia and then to the cerebral commissure
(see Fig. 4A). The bipolar cells in the foot projected to the
neuropil of the pedal ganglia (Fig. 3B).
The ﬁrst CAergic cell bodies appearing within the
ganglia of the developing central nervous system (CNS)
were detected at E75 (after metamorphosis was complete;
Fig. 4A). Three CAergic cells were present within each
cerebral ganglion. One cell body was located laterally on
the ventral surface and sent its single axon into the
cerebral–pedal connective (for a detailed description of
these cells, see Croll et al., 1998). The perikarya of two
other cells lay close together in a more medial position on
the ventral surface of the ganglion. The processes from
these latter cells could be traced to the central neuropil (for
a detailed description of these cells, also see Croll et al.,
1998). Only a single, unpaired CAergic cell was present in
the right pedal ganglion at this time (Fig. 4A). Based on its
position in the ganglion and its caudal projection to the
developing mantle, we identiﬁed this cell as RPeD1, a
DAergic neuron that has been well studied in the adult
Lymnaea CNS (Haydon and Winlow, 1981).
By E85, a population of CAergic neurons was ﬁrst
detected in the cerebral ganglia between the bases of the
medial and lateral labial nerves. Fibers from these cells
entered the ipsilateral cerebrobuccal connective and pro-
jected toward the medial regions of the buccal ganglia. The
numbers of CAergic peripheral neurons also increased
dramatically at this stage of embryogenesis. Atlhough the
majority of such cells remained concentrated in the ante-
rior margin of the foot and around the lips (Fig. 4B),
Chromatographic determination of monoaminergic con-
tents of developing embryos. A: Chromatographs show detection of 10
pmol of each synthetic monoaminergic standards: norepinephrine
(NE), epinephrine (E), dopamine (DA), isoproterenol (IS), and seroto-
nin (5HT). B,C: Detection of dopamine, isoproterenol (internal stan-
dard), and serotonin in extracts of embryos at stages embryonic (E) 45
and E85. D: Mean content (6 S.E.) in embryos at different stages of
development. Curve represents a computer-generated (Statistica,
StatSoft, Tulsa, OK) least-squares ﬁt of data points.
sufficient numbers of isolated CAergic cells were also
scattered around other regions of the body wall, so that
several peripheral nerves were distinctly labeled (Fig. 4C).
Only a single CAergic cell was seen in each of the tentacles
occupying a position posterolateral to the eye.
The development of the CAergic system from early to
late embryogenesis was characterized by continuous cell
addition. Only one event stood in relief to this consistent
background: at E85–90, the TAC neurons were not de-
tected in any of specimens (n 5 180) examined with either
immunocytochemistry or glyoxylate- or aldehyde-induced
ﬂuorescence techniques (Fig. 4D).
By E95, the lips had expanded to become ﬂaps of skin
anterior and lateral to the mouth. Numerous CAergic cells
were located within these lips (Fig. 4D). The long processes
of these cells projected to the cerebral ganglia through the
labial nerves. Similar cells were also found within the
esophagus just caudal to the buccal mass (Fig. 4E). The
long processes of these latter cells appeared to project into
the buccal ganglia and end within the neuropil. By this
stage, several additional CAergic neurons could also be
observed in the central ganglia. A single neuron appeared
on the dorsal surface of each cerebral ganglion and sent its
axon process to the cerebral commissure. One or two
groups of CAergic neurons were seen in each pedal gan-
glion near the anterior pedal nerves. Interestingly, only
one of these groups was ever observed to exhibit TH-lir,
whereas two groups were often demonstrated by glyox-
ylate- and aldehyde-induced blue-green ﬂuorescence (for a
detailed description of these cells, see Croll et al., 1998).
Within the buccal ganglia two CAergic cells were reliably
detected along the posterior margin, and a single medial
cell was occasionally found along the anterior margin and
a cluster of lateral somata were observed (Fig. 4E).
At approximately E15, the embryos began to rotate
continuously within the egg capsule (Fig. 5A). By E45, the
veligers began to modulate the speed of rotation and often
stop momentarily after contacting the wall of the egg
capsule. During metamorphosis (E55–70), the embryos
gradually switched from rotational behavior to using the
foot as a locomotory organ to glide over the inner surface of
the egg capsule (Fig. 6). At this point, they also produced
frequent and seemingly spontaneous contractions of the
The ﬁrst occasional movements (approximately 5 move-
ments every 10 minutes) of the buccal mass were observed
at stage E80 (Figs. 5B, 6). These movements usually
involved only radular protraction without full retraction
past the normal resting position. By E90, full excursion of
the radula (involving both protraction and retraction) was
observed, and these rasping movements occurred more
Late trochophore or early veliger stage embryos (E32–E35).

frequently. By stage E95, episodes of continuous rhythmic
rasping with durations of 0.5–4.0 minutes and frequencies
of 11–15 rasps per minute were observed.
Near E100, the embryos nearly ﬁlled the entire volume
of their egg capsules and engaged in locomotion very
rarely. Periods of general inactivity lasting up to 30
minutes alternated with equally long periods of uninter-
rupted, rhythmic buccal activity. The frequency of the
observed movements at this late stage of development was
similar (11–15 rasps/minute) to that observed at E95.
Hatching occurred when the embryo rasped a hole through
In addition to general activity and buccal movements,
we also quantiﬁed developmental changes in respiratory
behavior (Fig. 7). The pneumostome was ﬁrst observed at
around stage E65, at which point it continuously remained
fully open. By E70, occasional, partial closing movements
were ﬁrst detected, but it was not until around E75 that
the ﬁrst complete closure of the pneumostome was ob-
served. At ﬁrst, these closures were only brief (3–15
seconds), and they occurred irregularly at an average
frequency of 4.4 every 5 minutes. By E85, 25% of the
embryos kept the pneumostome closed during the entire
5-minute observation period. For those that continued to
alternate opening and closing movements, the typical
duration of closure ranged from 30 seconds to 2.5 minutes
and occurred at an average frequency of 3.6 every 5
minutes. By E90, 60% of the embryos maintained their
pneumostome closed; for those that continued to alternate
movements, the duration of closure ranged from 30 sec-
onds to 4.0 minutes, with an average frequency of 2.6 every
5 minutes. Respiratory behaviors did not appear to corre-
late with any speciﬁc locomotory behavior at any stage of
embryonic development. At stages E95–100, 100% of em-
bryos kept the pneumostome closed, as was expected for a
Among the CAs, only DA was detectable in the embry-
onic central and peripheral nervous systems of Lymnaea
with our HPLC procedures. Furthermore, developmental
changes in DA content correlate with the appearance of
major populations of cells that exhibit both reactivity to
antibodies raised against TH and to glyoxylate- and alde-
hyde-induced green ﬂuorescence (for a summary of devel-
opmental changes in the distribution of CAergic neurons,
see Fig. 7). Developmental changes in DA content occur in
three distinct phases: (1) an early, gradual increase, (2) a
leveling of content during mid-embryogenesis, and (3) a
subsequent increase that starts gradually and then rap-
idly accelerates in the ﬁnal day before hatching. Interest-
Catecholaminergic neurons in embryos during metamorpho-
sis (E55–75). A: Only peripheral cells exhibit tyrosine hydroxylaselike
immunoreactivity (TH-lir) at E55. Transient anterior catecholaminer-
gic (TAC) cell bodies are indicated. Arrows mark the somata of cells on
the ventral surface of the foot. B: TH-lir at the anterior region of the
foot at E60. Larger arrows indicate bipolar cells. Smaller arrows
indicate the long processes of these cells, which converge and project to
the pedal ganglion (PeG). C: Glyoxylate-induced ﬂuorescence in
somata (larger arrows) and axons (smaller arrows) at the edge of the
foot at E70. D: A single bipolar cell (arrow) in the tentacle (Tent) near
the eye (Eye) at E75. Scale bars 5 50 µm in A, 12 µm in B, 10 µm in C,
ingly, the ﬁrst detectable DA predates the ﬁrst detectable
CAergic neurons, the TAC cells. Such ‘‘prenervous’’appear-
ance of monoamines has, however, be described in echino-
derms and has been attributed to either maternal origins
or synthetic activity in nonneural tissue (Buznikov, 1987).
Appearance of the TAC cells and the growth of their
neurites beneath the apical plate presumably contribute to
increasing DA content during E30–40. During mid-
embryogenesis, no new CAergic neurons are added to the
embryo, and the total DA content remains constant. Start-
ing at around E55, a few small peripheral neurons are
detected but appear to add little to the total DA content of
the embryo. As development proceeds, however, the addi-
tion of these peripheral cells accelerates, and the total DA
content also begins to increase, slowly at ﬁrst but then
rapidly as the embryo approaches hatching and additional
CAergic cells are added to both the peripheral and central
A most remarkable feature of the CAergic phenotype
that stands in stark contrast to other transmitter pheno-
types (e.g., serotonergic, FMRFaminergic, octopaminergic)
studied thus far in gastropods (see introduction for refer-
ences) is that the vast majority of the neurons appearing
during embryogenesis are located in the periphery (for a
Tyrosine hydorxylaselike immunoreactivity (TH-lir) in em-
bryonic neurons after the completion of metamorphosis (E75–95).

lip project to the cerebral ganglia (CG), and those along the ventral
surface project to the pedal ganglia in which RPeD1 resides. Transient
anterior catecholaminergic (TAC) cells with their neurites are also
visible under the apical plate. B: Fluorescent bipolar cells along the
lips. C: TH-lir in cells in the lips and throughout ventral regions of the
foot at E95. Processes from the cells in the foot converge on the pedal
ganglia (PG). The dorsal eyes are out of the focal plane of this
photograph. D: Dorsal view of anterior regions of the head shows

CATECHOLAMINERGIC CELLS IN EMBRYONIC SNAILS
summary of the distributions of CAergic neurons during
embryonic development, see Fig. 8). The ﬁrst pair of
CAergic cells, the TAC neurons, is located under the apical
plate, which lies dorsal to the mouth. The timing of
appearance and the general morphology of these cells is
consistent with a previous description of FMRFamide-like
immunoreactive cells in Lymnaea (Croll and Voronezhs-
kaya, 1996a). In fact, these same cells double label with
antisera raised against FMRFamide and TH and also
exhibit slight reactivity to antibodies raised against 5-HT
(E.E. Voronezhskaya and R.P. Croll, unpublished results).
In addition to containing detectable levels of three poten-
tial neurotransmitters, the TAC cells are also distinctive in
their morphology. The ciliated, short, stout process from
the soma of each cell is highly suggestive of a primary
sensory function. The role of the axonal terminals is less
clear because the function of the innervated apical plate
itself is uncertain; a sensory role might be suggested
Example recordings of embryonic behavior. A: Examples of
photovoltaic recordings of embryonic movements at E35–75. At E35,
embryos rotate continuously while producing regular sinusoidal record-
ings (1). By E55, rotation becomes more irregular and is interrupted
by pauses (4) and periods during which the animals crawl around the
inner surface of the egg capsule (2). By E60, such crawling revolutions
predominate. By E75, crawling becomes more irregular, and local body
contractions (3) become more frequent. B: Dual traces of photovoltaic
recordings (upper traces) and simultaneous occurrences of rasping
(biting) behavior (lower traces) at E80–95 as crawling revolutions
occur less and rasping behavior increases in frequency.
Mean percentages of embryos rotating, crawling, and/or rasping at E25–100. Error bars
indicate standard errors based on n 5 25.
because ciliated, apical organs of different molluscs are
generally considered to be sensory (Kempf et al., 1997), but
a motor role may also be consistent with reported effects of
both CAs and 5-HT on locomotory cilia of molluscan larvae
(Koshtoyants et al., 1961; Diefenbach et al., 1991; Berias
The locations and aspects of the morphology of the TAC
neurons are very similar to those of the transient 5-HT-
containing cells (TS1) described in the embryogenesis of
planorbid species of pulmonates (Voronezhskaya and Ele-
kes 1993; these cells were initially reported to be the
embryonic forms of the ‘‘metacerebral giant’’ C1 cells by
Goldberg and Kater, 1989). The TS1 and TAC cells are
located dorsal to the mouth but remain distinctly outside
the cerebral ganglia (Voronezhskaya and Elekes, 1993).
Both cells are characterized by relatively large, vase-
shaped somata that bear cilia at the end of what appear to
be primary dendrites that penetrate the body wall (Voron-
ezhskaya and Elekes, 1993; E.E. Voronezhskaya and R.P.
Croll, unpublished results). Together, these ﬁndings sug-
gest that the cells represent evolutionary vestiges of an
apical organ that has been described in several species of
gastropods. Marois and Carew (1997c) and Kempf et al.
(1997) described ciliated monoaminergic cells in the apical
organs in a variety of opisthobranch gastropods, and Croll
et al. (1997) described a strikingly similar pattern of
monoaminergic neurons in the apical region of bivalve
molluscs. The fact that both the TAC cells of Lymnaea and
the TS1 cells of planorbid pulmonates disappear before
hatching is also consistent with the ﬁnding that the apical
organs of other species of gastropods disappear after
metamorphosis (Marois and Carew, 1997a). Interestingly,
however, the TAC and the TS1 cells differ from each other
not only in their transmitter contents but also in their
morphologies. The TAC cells terminate under the ciliated
apical plate. Planorbid snails, however, lack such a large
ciliated plate (E.E. Voronezhskaya and R.P. Croll, unpub-
lished results), and the TS1 cells instead terminate under
patches of ciliated cells on the developing foot (Voronezhs-
kaya and Elekes, 1993). Further work is needed to deter-
mine the evolutionary bases for the similarities and the
differences between the TAC cells and the TS1 cells in such
The next populations of CAergic cells to appear during
ontogeny are also located in the peripheral regions of the
embryonic body. The ﬁrst of these bipolar cells are detected
in the epithelia of the foot and tentacles at the beginning of
metamorphosis. Similar cells appear later in the lips.
These numerous cells are characterized by subepithelial
somata and what appear to be dendrites that penetrate the
body wall to terminate on the outer surface of the embryo.
The axonal processes of these cells project centrally and
contribute to the neuropil of the cerebral and pedal
ganglia. In fact, because these cells predate any central
CAergic cells, they provide the only source of CAs to the
developing CNS during a signiﬁcant proportion of embryo-
genesis. Although the peripheral, bipolar neurons appear
to be sensory cells, the modality they serve is unclear.
These cell populations do, however, persist through postem-
bryonic life (Croll et al., 1998), and their central projec-
tions apparently continue to account for a large proportion
Central CAergic neurons, located ﬁrst within the cere-
bral and pedal ganglia, appear at E75, a stage relatively
late in embryogenesis after the completion of metamorpho-
sis. Additional groups of neurons in the cerebral, pedal,
and buccal ganglia start to contain detectable levels of CAs
closer to hatching. These central CAergic neurons and
neuronal clusters can be subsequently tracked from hatch-
ing through to adulthood (Croll et al., 1999). The behav-
ioral and physiological actions of certain of these central
neurons have been demonstrated or hypothesized previ-
ously (Syed et al., 1990). Thus, in addition to providing a
description of novel developmental processes (e.g., the ﬁrst
account of the embryonic development of peripheral neu-
rons within a mollusc), the present study also permits a
rough correlation between the ontogeny of behavior and
the ontogeny of the CAergic neuronal phenotype.
The purpose of the behavioral aspect of the present
study was not only to provide measures that relate to the
development of CAergic control but also to provide a
behavioral context for neurogenesis. For example, consis-
tent with the present ﬁndings and those of previous
reports (Diefenbach et al., 1991), the initially continuous
rotational movements of the early embryo appear to be
independent of neuronal control, and no obvious changes
in our measures occurred with the appearance of the TAC
cells. Instead, changes in rotational behavior, which began
by around E45, are probably more likely due to the
appearance of other cells such as the ﬁrst serotonergic
neurons that innervate ciliary patches on the foot (Marois
and Croll, 1992; Voronezhskaya and Elekes, 1993). Like-
wise, the initiation of foot attachment and crawling are
also correlated with the appearances of additional seroton-
ergic neurons (Marois and Croll, 1992). It must be empha-
sized, however, that the appearance of cells during develop-
ment may not indicate their immediate usage but rather
steps in the assemblage of neuronal circuits that only
become functional when they are fully completed. Also,
some neurons may be involved in covert behavioral or
physiological processes. For example, numerous reports
have indicated a potential role for CAs in the induction of
metamorphosis of different molluscs including Lymnaea
(Coon and Bonar, 1986; Voronezhskaya et al., 1992, 1993;
Pires et al., 1997). Therfore, TAC cells may be involved in
Mean percentage of time that the pneumostome was
observed to be open during 5-minute periods examined at E65–95.
Error bars indicate standard errors.
CATECHOLAMINERGIC CELLS IN EMBRYONIC SNAILS
triggering developmental processes rather than directly
controlling an overt behavior.
The appearance of numerous, presumably sensory, cells
in peripheral regions, such as the foot and tentacles,
correlates with the development of localized contractions
of the body wall and more complex movements such as
turning. The ﬁrst sporadic, buccal movements, consisting
only of repetitive protractions without complete interven-
ing retractions, were observed at stage E85, when the ﬁrst
CAergic axonal processes from cerebrobuccal neurons ap-
pear in the buccal ganglia. Serotonergic ﬁbers from the C1
cells are also present in the buccal region by this time
(Marois and Croll, 1992). CAergic cell bodies in these
ganglia are not yet visible at this stage, although the
possibility cannot be excluded that such cells may already
be differentiated but possess undetectable amounts of
transmitter. The feeding cycle of Lymnaea matures by
stage E95, when the ﬁrst CAergic neurons can be detected

in the buccal ganglia. This is also the time that octopamin-
ergic neurons can ﬁrst be detected in the ganglia (Elekes et
al., 1996). The signiﬁcant increase of DA concentration at
the time of hatching detected by HPLC correlates well
with the role of DA in stimulating feeding (Wieland and
Gelperin, 1983; Trimble and Barker, 1984; Kyriakides and
McCrohan, 1989) and inhibiting locomotion (Sakharov and
We also investigated the ontogeny of respiratory behav-
ior because of the well-documented involvement of the
dopaminergic RPeD1 neuron in the generation of the
motor program. Syed et al. (1990) demonstrated that
interconnections between RPeD1 and other central neu-
rons are sufficient to generate the basic rhythmicity that
appears to underlie respiration. In the present study, we
report that the ﬁrst movements of the pneumostome
precede the ﬁrst detection of RPeD1 in development.
Complete closure of the aperture and the initiation of
rhythmicity, however, correlate with the appearance of the
cell. The development of a more regular rhythm and
subsequent suppression of that rhythmicity, as might be
expected for a submerged snail, are likely to be due to the
development of additional cells to the underlying neuronal
In summary, the chromatographic, histological, and
behavioral analyses presented in this report provide com-
prehensive descriptions of the development of CAergic
cells and CA-dependent behaviors within a well-studied
model organism, the pond snail Lymnaea stagnalis. Fur-
thermore, recent evidence suggests that both the ontogeny
of peripheral CAergic cells and qualitative changes in CA
content during development may be broadly generalizable
to other molluscs (Croll et al., 1997; Pires et al., 1997).
Thus, the present study can provide a basic foundation for
understanding potential roles of CAs and in particular DA
as developmental signals and in the generation of speciﬁc
behaviors in molluscs. CAs and their synthetic enzymes
can also provide reliable markers for the study of the
developmental origins and differentiation of both central
and peripheral neurons; peripheral neurons have re-
mained completely unexplored to date in molluscs. The
study of such fundamental topics within this phylum will
in turn offer novel perspectives into the functional and
developmental processes that have shaped the evolution of
nervous systems across the animal kingdom.
